revision.comment: Bot: remove [[Category:Sn crud]] (crud category cleanup) [[github:EmmaLeonhart/shintowiki-scripts/actions/runs/23984597483|manual triggered pipeline]]
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Fukurokuju]][[en:Fukurokuju]][[eo:Fukurokuju]][[fa:فوکوروکوجی]][[fr:Fukurokuju]][[id:Fukurokuju]][[it:Fukurokuju]][[ja:福禄寿]][[lb:Fukurokuju]][[pl:Fukurokuju]][[ru:Фукурокудзю]][[simple:Fukurokuju]][[sv:Fukurokuju]][[tr:Fukurokuju]][[uk:Фукурокудзю]][[vi:Fukurokuju]][[zh:福祿壽 (日本神祇)]]
{{Short description|God of the Japanese mythology}} {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}}
, [[:en:Aichi|Aichi]] Prefecture]] In {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}, '''Fukurokuju''' ({{lang|ja|福禄寿}}; from {{ill|Japanese language|en|Japanese language|qid=Q5287|lt=Japanese|1=Japanese language}} ''fuku'', "happiness"; ''roku'', "wealth"; and ''ju'', "longevity") is one of the [[Seven Lucky Gods]] in [[Japanese mythology]].<ref name="Goldberg">{{cite journal|last1=Goldberg|first1=Joshua|title=In praise of darkness: the 'Hands-on Japan' exhibition|journal=Museum International|date=24 April 2009|volume=33|issue=3|pages=187–193|doi=10.1111/j.1468-0033.1981.tb01961.x}}</ref> It has been theorized that he is a Japanese {{ill|Cultural assimilation|en|Cultural assimilation|qid=Q193579|lt=assimilation|1=Cultural assimilation}} of the Chinese [[:en:Three Star Gods|Three Star Gods]] (Fu Lu Shou) embodied in one deity. Most related in appearance to the Chinese star god Shou, he is the God of wisdom and {{ill|Longevity|en|Longevity|qid=Q1066907|lt=Longevity|1=Longevity}}.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Davis |first1=F. Hadland |title=Myths and Legends of Japan |date=1912 |publisher=George G. Harrap & Company |location=London |page=388 |url=https://www.globalgreyebooks.com/content/books/ebooks/myths-and-legends-of-japan.pdf#page=448 |access-date=18 April 2020 |archive-date=12 April 2020 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200412091341/https://www.globalgreyebooks.com/content/books/ebooks/myths-and-legends-of-japan.pdf#page=448 |url-status=dead }}</ref> According to some, before attaining divinity, he was a Chinese hermit of the {{ill|Song dynasty|en|Song dynasty|qid=Q7462|lt=Song dynasty|1=Song dynasty}} and a reincarnation of the [[:en:Taoist|Taoist]] Deity, {{ill|Xuanwu (god)|en|Xuanwu (god)|qid=Q704070|lt=Xuantian Shangdi|1=Xuanwu (god)}}. It is said that during his human incarnation, he was a {{ill|Sennin|en|Sennin|qid=Q10861341|lt=Sennin|1=Sennin}}; a immortal who could exist without eating food.
Fukurokuju probably originated from an old Chinese tale about a mythical Chinese Taoist hermit sage renowned for performing miracles in the Northern Song period (960–1127). In China, this hermit (also known as [[Jurōjin]]) was thought to embody the celestial powers of the south polar star. Fukurokuju was not always included in the earliest representations of the Seven in Japan. He was instead replaced by [[Kisshōten]] (goddess of fortune, beauty, and merit). He is now, however, an established member of the Seven Lucky Gods.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Sykes |first1=Egerton |editor1-last=Kendall |editor1-first=Alan |title=Who's Who in Non-Classical Mythology |date=1993 |publisher=Oxford University Press, Inc. |location=New York |page=73}}</ref>
He is sometimes confused with [[Jurōjin]], another of the Several Gods of Fortune, who by some accounts is Fukurokuju's grandson and by other accounts inhabits the same body as Fukurokuju.<ref name="graham">{{cite book|last=Graham|first=Patricia|title=Faith and Power in Japanese Buddhist Art, 1600-2005|year=2007|publisher=University of Hawaii Press|isbn=978-0-8248-3126-4|pages=110|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=EBABpv4uXYgC&pg=PA110}}</ref> As such, the two are often confused.<ref name="nihon">{{cite encyclopedia|encyclopedia=Nihon Daihyakka Zensho (Nipponika) |title=寿老人 |url=http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |access-date=2013-01-12 |year=2013 |publisher=Shogakukan |location=Tokyo |language=ja |trans-title=Jurōjin |oclc=153301537 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |archive-date=2007-08-25 }}</ref>
Fukurokuju is usually portrayed as bald with long whiskers and an elongated forehead.<ref name="Goldberg"/> He is said to be an incarnation of the "Southern Polestar" (南极老人, literally {{ill|Old Man of the South Pole|en|Old Man of the South Pole|qid=Q2200117|lt=Old Man of the South Pole|1=Old Man of the South Pole}}), also known as the star {{ill|Canopus|en|Canopus|qid=Q12189|lt=Canopus|1=Canopus}} outside of Asia. The sacred book tied to his staff either contains the lifespan of every person on earth or a magical scripture. He is accompanied by a {{ill|Crane (bird)|en|Crane (bird)|qid=Q25365|lt=crane|1=Crane (bird)}} and a {{ill|Turtle|en|Turtle|qid=Q223044|lt=Turtle|1=Turtle}}, which are considered to be symbols of longevity. He is also sometimes accompanied by a black {{ill|Deer|en|Deer|qid=Q23390|lt=Deer|1=Deer}} (ancient legends say a deer turns black if it is over 2000 years old).
He is the only member of the Seven Lucky Gods credited with the ability to resurrect the dead.
==See also== *{{ill|Kuebiko|en|Kuebiko|qid=Q6441847|lt=Kuebiko|1=Kuebiko}} *{{ill|Old Man of the South Pole|en|Old Man of the South Pole|qid=Q2200117|lt=Old Man of the South Pole|1=Old Man of the South Pole}}
==References== {{reflist}}
== Bibliography == *{{Commonscat-inline|Fukurokuju}}
* {{cite book|last1=Ashkenazi|first1=Michael|title=Handbook of Japanese Mythology|url=https://archive.org/details/handbookofjapane0000ashk|url-access=registration|date=2003|publisher=ABC-CLIO|location=Santa Barbara, California|isbn=978-1-57607-467-1}} * {{cite book|last=Chiba|first=Reiko |title=Seven Lucky Gods of Japan|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=9THRAgAAQBAJ&pg=PT7|date=28 August 2012|publisher=Tuttle Publishing|isbn=978-1-4629-0420-4}}
{{jmyth navbox long}} {{Japan-myth-stub}}
